And so possible Sidney's gonna consult with him about fixing these problems. Now to the best of my knowledge Sidney's never run a restaurant, she's never owned a restaurant and probably what she knows specifically about restaurants is how to order wine. But she goes and looks and I'm listening on the phone as she's telling me what she's seen. And it's a long list of things that common sense tells you are wrong, are incongruent with the objectives that he stated for this restaurant. And it's a perfectly good list of things that need to be changed. Didn't require owning a restaurant, didn't require going to the restaurant management institute, didn't require anything. Just requires walking in with an open mind, an objective attitude about the place, some common sense, watching people and how they're reacting and saying why in the hell would they be doing that? Doesn't require much more than that. Then it will require asking a bunch of questions so that the guy kind of feels like he's figuring it out for himself and then everybody's gonna be happy. So it is not about lack of experience. You should worry if you have no common sense. That would be a legitimate problem. But lack of experience, no. Let's see. Every field I've, in a 90 day period I became the most in demand consultant in the television infomercial industry. Never produced an infomercial, misspelled infomercial, couldn't run it, couldn't actually do anything physically like run a camera or anything. If I touch it, it breaks. No previous TV experience, no previous movie industry experience. Not about that. It's about common sense. Right? Well, let's see. If there's no offer, that's probably not good because people are not going to run to their phones. Right? I mean, common sense. But people put TV shows together all the time with no offer. Concerns over your financial status versus the clients. We're gonna talk about that quite a bit. Well, all my clients make more money than I do. How dare I tell them? We're gonna talk about that in a second. Personal hangups about money. We're talking about that. Invisible displays of weakness. We can't talk about that. So, let's jump to 20. Experience not required. Where am I? Lost my damn, here we go. Okay. This is from one of the books you should read if you haven't. It's called, Selling the Invisible. A good book about selling intangibles. Here's the big point. In most professional services, you are really not selling expertise because your expertise is assumed and because your prospect can't evaluate your expertise anyway. So, it's assumed if you make it assumed and he has no way to evaluate your expertise anyway until you actually deliver. And because most of the delivery does not revolve around expertise, it revolves around asking questions and it revolves around common sense, he'll be fine with the amount of expertise that you have. Here's my platinum list. Rory Fatt, largest coaching business in the restaurant industry, largest information marketing business in the restaurant industry, has exactly the same qualifications Sidney does. Never owned a restaurant, never ran a restaurant, never managed a restaurant, never built a successful restaurant. I don't even think he ever worked in a restaurant. And I don't think he knows how to order the right wine. So, actually she's got a leg up. But, I mean, his actual career experience prior to starting restaurant marketing systems was A, working as a salesman for a food service broker calling on restaurants and starting a business that delivered pre-cooked meals, frozen dinners to homes. Let's see, Bill and Steve Harrison, publicity experts. This year, a million dollars in coaching people about how to make themselves famous. Nobody ever asks, how come you haven't made yourself famous?
